meta.certificationName: AWS Certified Machine Learning Engineer – Associate
meta.title: AWS-MLE-4: Data Preparation, Serving Choices, and Operational Readiness

domains.name: Deployment and Orchestration of ML Workflows

questions.stem: An implementation review is underway. An ML platform owner wants users to have only the permissions required to build, train, and deploy approved models. Which security principle should guide the IAM design?

options.text: Rely on network ACLs instead of IAM policies
Use one shared root account for all data scientists

questions.explanation: **Apply least-privilege IAM permissions** is correct because AWS recommends granting only the permissions required for each role and workflow. Granting full admin access or relying solely on account-wide or network controls weakens security and governance.
questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AIAmazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/security_iam_id-based-policy-examples.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A machine learning team needs to explore, clean, and transform tabular training data with a visual workflow before training in Amazon SageMaker. Which SageMaker capability BEST fits this requirement?
questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler** is correct because it is designed for data preparation and feature engineering workflows before training. **Model Monitor** is for post-deployment monitoring, **Inference Recommender** benchmarks inference options, and **Ground Truth** focuses on data labeling.
questions.reference: [Prepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AIPrepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **govern model versions with approval states before promotion**. Which service or feature is the best fit?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because it stores versioned model packages and approval status for governed promotion workflows

**B** is incorrect because it is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets

**C** is incorrect because it is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario

**D** is incorrect because it does not act as the central online/offline feature repository for SageMaker ML features

[SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)
questions.reference: [SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A team needs versioned records of trained models, approval states, lineage, and a controlled handoff from experimentation to production. Which SageMaker feature addresses this need MOST directly?
questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Registry** is correct because it manages model versions, approval status, lineage, and deployment governance. **Neo** optimizes models for target hardware, **EventBridge Scheduler** schedules events, and **EMR Studio** is unrelated to model lifecycle governance.
questions.reference: [Model Registration Deployment with Model Registry - Amazon SageMaker AIModel Registration Deployment with Model Registry - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem:
A model receives occasional very large payloads and can take several minutes to produce each prediction.

Which hosting option is BEST?

options.text: Real-time endpoint with the smallest instance
Batch Transform only if clients need immediate responses
Serverless inference with a strict sub-second expectation

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because asynchronous inference is designed for large payloads or long processing times.

**B** is incorrect because small real-time endpoints are a poor fit for long-running requests.

**C** is incorrect because Batch Transform is offline rather than request/response.

**D** is incorrect because serverless is not the preferred choice for long-running asynchronous jobs.
questions.reference: [Asynchronous inference - Amazon SageMaker AIAsynchronous inference - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/async-inference.html)

questions.stem:
A team is reviewing its approach. A data science team needs a managed way to run feature engineering and evaluation code before training begins.

Which SageMaker capability is the BEST fit?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because SageMaker Processing is the managed service for preprocessing and evaluation jobs.

**A** is incorrect because Batch Transform is for offline inference, not pre-training processing.

**C** is incorrect because automatic model tuning searches hyperparameters rather than running preprocessing code.

**D** is incorrect because Model Monitor is for post-deployment or ongoing monitoring.
questions.reference: [Data transformation workloads with SageMaker Processing - Amazon SageMaker AIData transformation workloads with SageMaker Processing - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/processing-job.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **store reusable ML features for both training and online inference**. Which service or feature is the best fit?
questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because it provides managed online and offline feature storage to reduce training-serving skew

**A** is incorrect because it is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps

**B** is incorrect because it is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

**C** is incorrect because it does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs

[SageMaker Feature Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)
questions.reference: [SageMaker Feature Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)

questions.stem:
A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A responsible AI review requires bias analysis and explainability for model behavior before and once the model is deployed.

Which SageMaker feature BEST addresses that need?
questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because SageMaker Clarify provides bias detection and explainability workflows.

**A** is incorrect because AWS Config records resource configuration changes rather than ML explainability.

**B** is incorrect because Batch Transform performs offline predictions, not bias analysis.

**D** is incorrect because Athena queries data but does not provide model bias explanations.
questions.reference: [Pre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AIPre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-detect-data-bias.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **SageMaker Clarify**?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because teams should choose it when they need to analyze bias and explainability in ML workflows

**B** is incorrect because it is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets

**C** is incorrect because it is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario

**D** is incorrect because it is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

[SageMaker Clarify overview](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-fairness-and-explainability.html)
questions.reference: [SageMaker Clarify overview](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-fairness-and-explainability.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **AWS Glue**?
questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because teams should choose it when they need to crawl schemas and run serverless ETL over raw training data

**A** is incorrect because it is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

**B** is incorrect because it does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs

**C** is incorrect because it is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps

[AWS Glue concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html)
questions.reference: [AWS Glue concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **SageMaker Experiments**?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because teams should choose it when they need to compare training runs, parameters, metrics, and artifacts

**B** is incorrect because it is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps

**C** is incorrect because it is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

**D** is incorrect because it is not the main SageMaker capability for bias and explainability analysis

[SageMaker Experiments](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/experiments.html)
questions.reference: [SageMaker Experiments](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/experiments.html)

questions.stem: An implementation review is underway. A platform team wants a repeatable ML workflow that chains preprocessing, training, evaluation, approval, and deployment steps with lineage and auditability. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.text: AWS Step Functions Express Workflows only

questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Pipelines** is correct because it is purpose-built for orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows with SageMaker integrations and lineage. **Step Functions** can orchestrate general workflows but is not the purpose-built ML pipeline service described here. **Kinesis** and **AppConfig** do not provide ML pipeline orchestration.
questions.reference: [Pipelines - Amazon SageMaker AIPipelines - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **SageMaker Model Registry**?
questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because teams should choose it when they need to govern model versions with approval states before promotion

**A** is incorrect because it does not act as the central online/offline feature repository for SageMaker ML features

**B** is incorrect because it is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario

**C** is incorrect because it is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets

[SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)
questions.reference: [SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem:
A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A responsible AI review requires bias analysis and explainability for model behavior before and once the model is deployed.

Which SageMaker feature BEST addresses that need?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because SageMaker Clarify provides bias detection and explainability workflows.

**A** is incorrect because Athena queries data but does not provide model bias explanations.

**C** is incorrect because AWS Config records resource configuration changes rather than ML explainability.

**D** is incorrect because Batch Transform performs offline predictions, not bias analysis.
questions.reference: [Pre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AIPre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-detect-data-bias.html)

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A data scientist needs a managed way to run distributed training jobs, store model artifacts in Amazon S3, and track the resulting output for later deployment. Which SageMaker capability should be used?
questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker training jobs** are correct because they provide managed training infrastructure and produce model artifacts that can be deployed later. **CloudFront**, **Storage Gateway**, and **Route 53** do not perform model training.
questions.reference: [Model training - Amazon SageMaker AIModel training - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/train-model.html)

questions.stem:
A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A model receives occasional very large payloads and can take several minutes to produce each prediction.

Which hosting option is BEST?

options.text: Serverless inference with a strict sub-second expectation
Real-time endpoint with the smallest instance
Batch Transform only if clients need immediate responses

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because asynchronous inference is designed for large payloads or long processing times.

**A** is incorrect because serverless is not the preferred choice for long-running asynchronous jobs.

**B** is incorrect because small real-time endpoints are a poor fit for long-running requests.

**D** is incorrect because Batch Transform is offline rather than request/response.

questions.stem:
A team is reviewing its approach. A production team wants to detect data drift and model quality issues after a model is deployed.

Which managed SageMaker capability is the BEST fit?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Model Monitor is designed for ongoing checks such as data and model quality drift.

**A** is incorrect because Processing is for standalone jobs rather than deployment monitoring.

**C** is incorrect because Glue Studio is for data integration, not deployed model monitoring.

**D** is incorrect because notebooks are development tools, not managed drift monitors.
questions.reference: [Data and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AIData and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

questions.stem:
A team needs to compare training runs, parameters, and metrics from multiple SageMaker experiments.

Which feature is MOST directly designed for that purpose?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because SageMaker Experiments tracks and compares model-development runs.

**B** is incorrect because Batch Transform is for inference on batch data.

**C** is incorrect because EventBridge routes events rather than tracking experiments.

**D** is incorrect because Lambda layers are unrelated to experiment tracking.
questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker Experiments in Studio Classic - Amazon SageMaker AIAmazon SageMaker Experiments in Studio Classic - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/experiments.html)

options.text: AWS Step Functions Express Workflows only

questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Pipelines** is correct because it is purpose-built for orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows with SageMaker integrations and lineage. **Step Functions** can orchestrate general workflows but is not the purpose-built ML pipeline service described here. **Kinesis** and **AppConfig** do not provide ML pipeline orchestration.
questions.reference: [Pipelines - Amazon SageMaker AIPipelines - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.stem:
Which service or feature is most closely associated with the following need?

- Goal: crawl schemas and run serverless ETL over raw training data
- Constraint: use the managed vendor capability when possible

What should the team choose?
questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because it provides crawlers, ETL jobs, and a metadata catalog for serverless data preparation

**A** is incorrect because it is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps

**B** is incorrect because it is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

**D** is incorrect because it does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs

questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler** is correct because it is designed for data preparation and feature engineering workflows before training. **Model Monitor** is for post-deployment monitoring, **Inference Recommender** benchmarks inference options, and **Ground Truth** focuses on data labeling.
questions.reference: [Prepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AIPrepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

questions.stem: An ML platform owner wants users to have only the permissions required to build, train, and deploy approved models. Which security principle should guide the IAM design?

options.text: Rely on network ACLs instead of IAM policies
Use one shared root account for all data scientists

questions.explanation: **Apply least-privilege IAM permissions** is correct because AWS recommends granting only the permissions required for each role and workflow. Granting full admin access or relying solely on account-wide or network controls weakens security and governance.
questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AIAmazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/security_iam_id-based-policy-examples.html)

questions.stem: A model must handle unpredictable request spikes with minimal infrastructure management and no need to keep idle instances running. Which deployment pattern is MOST appropriate?

options.text: A large multi-AZ self-managed EC2 Auto Scaling group
Notebook instances exposed through a load balancer

questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference** is correct because it fits intermittent or spiky workloads without managing always-on instances. **Batch transform** is offline inference, and the EC2 or notebook options add unnecessary management overhead for this requirement.
questions.reference: [Deploy models for inference - Amazon SageMaker AIDeploy models for inference - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deploy-model.html)

options.text: AWS Step Functions Express Workflows only

questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Pipelines** is correct because it is purpose-built for orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows with SageMaker integrations and lineage. **Step Functions** can orchestrate general workflows but is not the purpose-built ML pipeline service described here. **Kinesis** and **AppConfig** do not provide ML pipeline orchestration.
questions.reference: [Pipelines - Amazon SageMaker AIPipelines - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because it provides bias detection and explainability capabilities for SageMaker ML systems

**A** is incorrect because it is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario

**B** is incorrect because it is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

**C** is incorrect because it is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets

questions.stem: A team needs to **analyze bias and explainability in ML workflows**. Which service or feature is the best fit?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because it provides bias detection and explainability capabilities for SageMaker ML systems

**A** is incorrect because it is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets

**C** is incorrect because it is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario

questions.stem: A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A production team wants automated checks for data quality drift and model quality issues after a model is deployed. Which SageMaker feature should they enable?
questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor** is correct because it continuously monitors deployed models for data quality, bias, feature attribution, and model quality signals. **JumpStart** provides solution starters, **DataBrew** prepares data, and **QuickSight Q** is unrelated to ML endpoint monitoring.
questions.reference: [Data and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AIData and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

questions.stem:
A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. An ML engineer must join and transform large raw datasets before model training, while maintaining a metadata catalog for repeatable ETL.

Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because AWS Glue is AWS’s managed ETL and catalog service for data preparation workflows.

**A** is incorrect because Model Registry governs model versions, not ETL.

**B** is incorrect because CodeDeploy is not the primary service for analytical ETL.

**D** is incorrect because CloudWatch is for monitoring.
questions.reference: [What is AWS Glue? - AWS GlueWhat is AWS Glue? - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/what-is-glue.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **search hyperparameter ranges against an objective metric**. Which service or feature is the best fit?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because it is the managed hyperparameter optimization feature in SageMaker

**B** is incorrect because it does not act as the central online/offline feature repository for SageMaker ML features

**C** is incorrect because it is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario

**D** is incorrect because it does not provide versioned model approval and governance for promotion

[SageMaker automatic model tuning](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/automatic-model-tuning.html)
questions.reference: [SageMaker automatic model tuning](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/automatic-model-tuning.html)

questions.stem:
Which service or feature is most closely associated with the following need?

- Goal: detect drift and quality issues once the model is deployed
- Constraint: use the managed vendor capability when possible

What should the team choose?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because it profiles baselines and monitors deployed models for data and model quality issues

**A** is incorrect because it serves a different ML lifecycle need than managed preprocessing jobs

**C** is incorrect because it is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets

**D** is incorrect because it is not the main SageMaker capability for bias and explainability analysis

**A** is incorrect because it does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs

questions.stem: A company wants to compute features once, reuse them across multiple models, and serve the same feature definitions for both training and online inference. Which service should the team use?
questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Feature Store** is correct because it centralizes feature definitions and supports both offline and online feature access. **S3 Glacier**, **Athena prepared statements**, and **CloudWatch Logs Insights** do not provide managed ML feature storage for training and serving reuse.

questions.stem: A data scientist needs a managed way to run distributed training jobs, store model artifacts in Amazon S3, and track the resulting output for later deployment. Which SageMaker capability should be used?
questions.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker training jobs** are correct because they provide managed training infrastructure and produce model artifacts that can be deployed later. **CloudFront**, **Storage Gateway**, and **Route 53** do not perform model training.
questions.reference: [Model training - Amazon SageMaker AIModel training - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/train-model.html)
